name: The Carter Center
mission: It seeks to prevent and resolve conflicts, enhance freedom and democracy, and improve health.
theoryOfChange: The Carter Center has a published "Peace Programs Strategic Plan" (2022-2027) which outlines its vision, goals, and objectives for preventing or mitigating the impact of war and conflict and promoting democracy, human rights, and the rule of law around the world. This strategic plan describes the world The Carter Center envisions, with countries and communities that are more peaceful, inclusive, participatory, empowered, equitable, transparent, and accountable. The plan also details new programming tools and approaches, including enhanced collaboration with local stakeholders, extensive integration and coordination across program areas for more impactful programs, and an analytical approach incorporating cross-cutting themes like gender, climate change, and health. It commits to implementing high-quality, evidence-based programs and includes a plan for creating an implementation plan to ensure that the vision, goals, and objectives can be measured and captured.
scoreSummary: The Carter Center Inc shows good impact and moderate alignment (major risk deductions applied).

evaluationSources: New York University Caregiver Intervention (NYUCI) demonstration projects

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Context dependent profile. Consider cause fit, risk profile, and evidence maturity before deciding.

keyConcerns.headline: 87% of expenses stay in the US despite international operations
keyConcerns.detail: Organization claims foreign operations but most spending remains domestic.

keyConcerns.headline: CEO compensation is 1.8% of revenue ($2,894,283 on $162,384,287)
keyConcerns.detail: For orgs with revenue >$50M, CEO pay above 1% of revenue warrants scrutiny.

keyConcerns.headline: Holds 91 months of reserves without clear endowment model
keyConcerns.detail: Organizations holding >36 months of reserves should publish deployment plans.

keyConcerns.headline: No outcome measurement or metrics tracking

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization offers a medium fit for donors, providing a reliable track record in advocacy and peace-building [2]. While it is a recognized venue for sadaqah, it maintains a broad humanitarian focus rather than a specialized niche for Muslim-specific causes [3].
dimension_explanations.impact: The Center demonstrates moderate impact through direct services and the establishment of international normative standards for credible elections [2]. Financial health is a primary driver of impact, characterized by a program expense ratio of 81.4% and a robust 91.0 months of working capital [4].

strengths: High program expense ratio of 81.4%, ensuring the majority of funds reach core missions

Highly efficient fundraising operations at $0.07 per $1 raised
baseline_narrative.summary: Founded on the mission to prevent conflict and enhance freedom, The Carter Center operates major initiatives including the Conflict Resolution Program and global Election Observation [2]. areas_for_improvement.context: The organization holds <cite id="8">91.0 months of operating reserves</cite>, which is significantly higher than the standard 6-12 month benchmark, suggesting room for more aggressive mission investment.
While program ratios are high, the Center could <cite id="10">publish consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for more accurate cost-per-beneficiary assessments.

bbb_assessment.summary: The organization currently does not meet BBB Wise Giving Alliance standards for charity accountability.

case_against.mitigation_notes: The Center's large endowment and investment income provide long-term sustainability for multi-decade disease eradication efforts.
risk_factors: <cite id="11">91.0 months of working capital</cite> may indicate inefficient capital deployment
<cite id="17">High domestic spending ratio</cite> relative to field operations
case_against.summary: The primary concerns involve <cite id="11">excessive financial reserves</cite> and a high domestic burn rate where <cite id="17">81.4% of expenses stay in the US</cite> despite an international mission.

confidence.notes: Financial data is highly reliable; impact data is verified for specific programs.

credibility.explanation: The Center is a highly credible institution <cite id="12">founded in 1982</cite> with a 44-year track record. It maintains <cite id="13">independent board oversight with 95% independent members</cite> and produces audited financial statements.
credibility.improvement: To improve credibility scores, the organization could <cite id="14">pursue a full BBB Wise Giving Alliance accreditation</cite>, as it currently meets 0 of 20 standards.

impact.explanation: Impact is driven by a <cite id="6">81.4% program ratio</cite> and direct service delivery in conflict zones. However, the <cite id="8">91.0 months of working capital</cite> is viewed as excessive, potentially slowing immediate impact.
impact.improvement: The Center should <cite id="10">publish a time-bound plan to deploy excess unrestricted reserves</cite> into mission delivery to maximize immediate humanitarian outcomes.

donor_fit_matrix.evidence_rigor: Grade B - Verified outcomes in disease eradication

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Funds research and local implementation partners for health and democracy initiatives.

rich_narrative.headline: The Carter Center Inc works to advance global peace and health by resolving conflicts and protecting democratic integrity through international election observation.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who value long-term institutional stability and want to support high-level diplomatic peace-building and global disease eradication.
donor_motivations: Value the legacy and diplomatic leverage of the Carter presidency
Support the total eradication of parasitic diseases
Believe in international election monitoring as a tool for democracy
Prefer donating to large, financially secure institutions
giving_considerations: Donations support a very large endowment and significant US-based operations
Impact is measured in decades (e.g., disease eradication) rather than immediate relief
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking to fulfill zakat obligations or those who prefer small, lean organizations with minimal cash reserves.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Verified outcomes in health (Guinea worm) and peace (election standards) provide strong evidence of effectiveness.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: The Center uses diplomatic mediation and election monitoring to create stable environments for health interventions, which in turn foster economic development.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that complex interventions in conflict zones actually lead to democratic stability and disease reduction.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While the Center has massive reserves, its global health eradication goals require sustained, multi-year funding commitments.
strategic_priorities: Conflict Resolution in the Middle East

organizational_capacity.geographic_reach: Global (Africa, Middle East, Latin America, Asia)

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="12">founding by a former U.S. President</cite> and its unique focus on <cite id="5">disease eradication alongside election monitoring</cite>.

strengths.detail: The organization directs <cite id="6">81.4% of its total expenses</cite> toward direct program services, exceeding industry benchmarks for humanitarian NGOs.
The Center maintains <cite id="8">91.0 months of working capital</cite>, providing a massive buffer to sustain long-term international projects through economic volatility.
The Center has successfully driven <cite id="5">Guinea worm cases down to 27</cite>, moving the world toward the second-ever eradication of a human disease.

strengths_deep_dive: The Carter Center's strength lies in its dual-track approach to global stability. Its peace programs focus on <cite id="4">33 detailed commitments for credible elections</cite>, while its health programs target neglected tropical diseases in <cite id="2">countries like Ghana and South Sudan</cite>. This work is supported by a robust financial engine; with <cite id="9">total assets exceeding $1.15 billion</cite>, the organization has the institutional weight to negotiate at the highest levels of government while maintaining <cite id="7">fundraising efficiency of $0.08 per dollar</cite>.
rich_narrative.summary: founded in 1982 by former U.S. President Jimmy Carter and Rosalynn Carter, The Carter Center is a non-profit organization committed to <cite id="1">preventing and resolving conflicts</cite>, enhancing freedom and democracy, and improving global health. The organization operates with a massive global footprint, maintaining <cite id="2">programs in countries including Ethiopia, Nigeria, Sudan, and Palestine</cite>. In the fiscal year 2023, the Center reported <cite id="3">total revenue of $153,773,756</cite>, supported by a diverse funding base of contributions and investment income. A hallmark of its peace-building work is the <cite id="4">establishment of 33 international normative standards for elections</cite>, which provides a framework for democratic integrity worldwide. On the health front, the Center has led the near-eradication of Guinea worm disease, reducing <cite id="5">human cases to just 27 globally</cite> as of recent reporting. The organization maintains a <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 81.4%</cite>, ensuring that the vast majority of its resources are directed toward its core missions of peace and health rather than administrative overhead. Despite its international reach, the Center manages its operations with high efficiency, reporting <cite id="7">fundraising costs of only $0.08 for every $1 raised</cite>.
